food_name: Bun Cha (Hanoi Grilled Pork Patties with Rice Vermicelli and Dipping Broth)

local_names.malay: Bun Cha Vietnam (Mi Bihun Babi Panggang Hanoi)

aliases: Bun Cha Vietnam (Mi Bihun Babi Panggang Hanoi)
grilled pork patties with rice vermicelli

english: grilled pork patties with rice vermicelli
malay: Bun Cha Vietnam (Mi Bihun Babi Panggang Hanoi)

food_subgroup: Hanoi-style grilled pork patties and belly in sweet-sour nuoc cham dipping broth, with rice vermicelli and fresh herbs

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for Bun Cha. Closest parent is a grilled meat with noodle and broth Vietnamese dish. No FoodOn URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Hanoi grilled pork patties and pork belly served in sweet-sour nuoc cham broth alongside rice vermicelli and fresh herbs
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0BQB is the closest FoodEx2 parent for Vietnamese pork noodle dishes; no exact Bun Cha code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Bun Cha as a specific dish.

main_ingredients: nuoc cham broth (fish sauce, vinegar, sugar, water, garlic)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Bun Cha set weighs approximately 450g, comprising 100g grilled pork (patties and belly), 150g rice vermicelli, 150g nuoc cham dipping broth, and 50g fresh herbs and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Bun Cha — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Bun Cha are adapted from the FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table, which covers Vietnamese composite dishes. Values represent the full mixed dish (pork, noodles, broth, herbs) per 100g edible portion. Where specific micronutrient data were not available in the FAO/INFOODS SEA table, values were cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for grilled pork and rice vermicelli components.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — Round rice vermicelli (bun) GI analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bun Cha as a composite dish. GI of 58 (medium) is estimated from round rice vermicelli (bun) GI range of 55–65 as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database, consistent with similar Vietnamese vermicelli dishes (e.g., Bun Bo Hue). Glycemic load of 43.1 is calculated from 74.3g available carbohydrate in the 450g standard serving (carbohydrate_g 16.5 × 4.5 = 74.25g) × GI 58 / 100 = 43.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and nuoc cham broth
hypertension.guidance: A standard 450g Bun Cha set delivers approximately 1,791mg sodium; however, because the nuoc cham is a dipping liquid rather than a consumed soup, actual sodium intake is typically lower than in pho or bun bo hue — patients with hypertension should limit broth consumption and request reduced fish sauce in the marinade.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (58) rice vermicelli with moderate glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: The 450g standard serving provides approximately 74g available carbohydrate with a glycemic load of 43.1; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the noodle portion to 100g (from 150g) to lower glycemic load to approximately 29, and pair with the protein-rich pork and fibre-containing herbs to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork belly and cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bun Cha contains 1.8g saturated fat and 42mg cholesterol per 100g; patients with cardiovascular disease should favour the lean minced pork patties over pork belly, limit portion to 300g, and ensure the dish is prepared with minimal added sugar in the marinade to reduce overall cardiometabolic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory herbs and zinc from grilled pork
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fresh herbs (perilla, mint, Vietnamese coriander) provide rosmarinic acid and quercetin with anti-inflammatory properties; grilled pork contributes 1.8mg zinc per 100g supporting immune function, though charcoal grilling generates minor AGE compounds that should be considered at high consumption frequency.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bún Chả is Hanoi's signature noodle dish and became internationally iconic after US President Barack Obama dined on it with Anthony Bourdain at a humble plastic-stool restaurant in Hanoi in 2016. The dish consists of two pork preparations: loose, juicy minced pork patties (chả viên) and thick pork belly slices (chả miếng), both marinated in fish sauce, sugar, garlic, and shallot, then grilled over charcoal until caramelised with dark grill marks and a smoky aroma. The grilled pork is immediately placed into a warm bowl of nuoc cham broth — a sweet-sour-savoury dipping liquid made from fish sauce, rice vinegar, sugar, water, garlic, and chilli — where it gently warms in the liquid. Separate plates of dry round rice vermicelli (bún) and a generous tower of fresh herbs (mint, perilla, Vietnamese coriander, bean sprouts, lettuce) are served alongside. The eating method is interactive: diners take noodles and herbs, dip and toss them in the broth, and eat with pieces of pork. The broth functions as a dipping liquid, not a soup, so less sodium is consumed than in pho or bun bo hue. Nutritionally, Bun Cha provides 142 kcal per 100g with 9.5g protein from lean and fatty pork, 16.5g carbohydrate from rice vermicelli, and moderate sodium at 398mg/100g. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free. The GI is estimated at 58 (medium) based on round rice vermicelli, with a glycemic load of 43.1 per 450g serving. Clinically, the dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions; sodium management is the primary concern for hypertensive patients, and noodle portion reduction is advised for Type 2 Diabetes management.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table, which covers Vietnamese composite dishes but does not provide an exact Bun Cha entry. Values represent a defensible composite estimate for the full dish (pork, noodles, broth, herbs) per 100g. GI is an analogous estimate from round rice vermicelli data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides an exact Bun Cha nutrient profile.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Bun Cha as a composite dish are not available in the FAO/INFOODS SEA table or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this preparation). GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on round rice vermicelli (bun) GI range 55–65 from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Bun Cha as a composite dish exists in the approved GI literature. Glycemic load of 43.1 is calculated as: available carbohydrate per serving (16.5 g/100g × 450g serving = 74.25g) × GI 58 / 100 = 43.1. The nuoc cham broth is a dipping liquid, not a consumed soup; actual sodium intake per eating occasion is typically lower than the per-100g figure implies. Halal status is not confirmed as pork is a primary ingredient — dish is not halal.
